stories.title: How Cult Kits built a brand around football shirt culture
stories.description: Co-founder David Furzer-Jones reflects on the rise of the vintage kit market, and how fandom, nostalgia and storytelling have fuelled the company’s growth
stories.articleText: Back in 2014, David Furzer-Jones was working in London on the creative agency scene while also running a football-themed website called Box2Box with his brother-in-law Josh Warwick, a journalist at the Telegraph. Warwick wrote an article in November that year titled ‘10 incontrovertible truths every Sunday league footballer knows’, covering relatable topics such as getting changed in a car park, having to wear mismatched kit and crooked opposition linesmen. It blew up, eventually catching the attention of Nike’s then-football communications director Jenny Simmons. “Box2Box was basically anything related to football that we liked or found interesting,” Furzer-Jones recalls. “At the time, football media was still very traditional, there wasn’t really a place that talked about football culturally in the way we wanted to. That article led to a conversation with Nike about what else we could do with it. So at that point, we kind of knew that we had something.” Around the same time, Warwick bought Furzer-Jones a vintage football shirt from Cult Kits, which began as a bedroom side hustle for their soon-to-be-business partner Rob Kocur. “Josh said, ‘This guy’s got an amazing selection of deadstock shirts, old kits…. Let’s see if he wants to sell some on the site,’” Furzer-Jones says. “Rob was making more money out of selling football shirts on eBay than he was from his day job … he was outgrowing the platform.” Top: Cult Kits x Just Eat pop-up event; Aove: David Furzer-Jones; All images © Cult Kits Sign in

stories.title: Police Raid on Religious Sect Sparks Hopes in Ongoing Search for Missing Woman - SSBCrack News
stories.description: In a turn of events that has reignited hope in the protracted search for Lisa Wiese, police recently raided the headquarters of the Ahmadi Religion of Peace
stories.articleText: In a turn of events that has reignited hope in the protracted search for Lisa Wiese, police recently raided the headquarters of the Ahmadi Religion of Peace and Light (AROPL) in Crewe, England. The operation involved around 500 officers and led to the arrest of twelve individuals on various charges, including modern-day slavery and human trafficking. Families of those who have disappeared from AROPL, particularly Wiese’s children, are now anxious for clarity regarding their mother’s fate. Wiese, a 30-year-old German national, was last heard from in March 2019 while she was on a trip to Kerala, India. Her final communication, an emotional email from a budget hotel, conveyed her love and longing for her two children, whom she referred to as her “best friends.” Two days later, she vanished without a trace. She became an AROPL member after converting to Islam in 2011 and had been living in various countries with the sect before her disappearance. Following her last known movements—where she reportedly stayed at a cliffside budget hotel overlooking Varkala beach—her activities ceased. Despite interactions with friends and family days before her disappearance, including requests for financial support, there has been no trace of Wiese since she deactivated her Gmail account shortly after sending the last messages to her loved ones. While police investigations were launched swiftly in Germany and India, and an individual linked to AROPL was identified for questioning, significant progress has been stymied by complications in international law enforcement. Wiese’s ex-husband, AbdelRahman Hashem, expressed concern that the current police inquiry in the UK, known as Operation Decker, is not broad enough to encompass Wiese’s case, despite prior interest from both German and Indian officers regarding the same AROPL member. Zverev takes first set against Mensik in semi-final at Roland Garros Solidion Technology Inc. Shares Surge Amid Breakthrough in Lithium Battery Technologies AbdelRahman remains steadfast in his pursuit for answers for his children, now aged 11 and 12. The psychological toll of their mother’s disappearance weighs heavily on him, as he navigates their grief and emotions. The recent police operation has stirred a mix of hope and despair in the family, especially for the children who are still holding onto hope that their mother might be found. In the aftermath of the raid, AbdelRahman stated, “Five hundred, that’s a lot of police officers. It’s like a very vast, vast difference,” indicating the disparity between the extensive resources committed to the current investigation and the ongoing challenge of resolving Wiese’s disappearance. He yearns for any information that may bring closure to his family, emphasizing the deep emotional impact of the unsolved mystery on his children’s lives. As investigations continue, the community remains on alert, hoping for a breakthrough that could shed light on the whereabouts of Lisa Wiese, who vanished seven years ago under circumstances that remain chillingly unclear.

stories.title: Cuotas, control y miedo. ‘No sabía que estaba en una secta porque nací en ella’: Hermanos revelan cómo operaba Power Mantra en Panamá antes del juicio de Operación Omega
stories.description: A pocos días del inicio del juicio por la llamada Operación Omega, dos hermanos que crecieron dentro de la organización conocida como Power Mantra, también identificada como “Sociedad Universal del Divino Mantra” y pr...
stories.articleText: Policiales Cuotas, control y miedo. ‘No sabía que estaba en una secta porque nací en ella’: Hermanos revelan cómo operaba Power Mantra en Panamá antes del juicio de Operación Omega Vendían en calles desde menores, debían cumplir cuotas de hasta $160 diarios y vivían bajo reglas estrictas. Hoy denuncian lo que, según su testimonio, ocurría dentro de Power Mantra. Publicado el: 24 de mayo del 2026, 01:46 am Por Emily Sánchez CORPRENSA MI DIARIO- “NOS HACÍAN VENDER DESDE NIÑOS”: EL RELATO QUE EXPONE A PRESUNTA SECTA POWER MANTRA Escuchar audio noticia A pocos días del inicio del juicio por la llamada Operación Omega , dos hermanos que crecieron dentro de la organización conocida como Power Mantra, también identificada como “Sociedad Universal del Divino Mantra” y presuntamente vinculada en otros países al nombre “ Misión Dorada”, decidieron hablar públicamente sobre lo que describen como años de control psicológico, explotación laboral y aislamiento. El proceso judicial iniciará el próximo 29 de junio de 2026 en Panamá y está relacionado con una investigación por presunta trata de personas en modalidad de explotación laboral. En enero de 2023, durante la Operación Omega , autoridades realizaron allanamientos en distintos puntos de Panamá y Coclé, rescataron a 28 presuntas víctimas y aprehendieron a varios investigados. Entre los hallazgos reportados públicamente estuvieron dinero en efectivo, equipos tecnológicos, mercancía que supuestamente era vendida por las víctimas y 43 aves exóticas encontradas en cautiverio en un apartamento allanado en Paitilla. Las víctimas entrevistadas En Exclusiva por Mi Diario son los hermanos Kishorachandra Arevalo Marrelli y Shandrakanty Arevalo. Él nació en Italia. Ella nació en Venezuela. Ambos poseen cédula panameña y crecieron dentro de la organización porque sus padres ya formaban parte del grupo antes de que nacieran. Kishorachandra, llegó a los 11 años a Panamá y su hermana en ese entonces tenía 15 años. La familia llegó invitada a un retiro espiritual en Playa Farallón y desde entonces sirvieron al gurú en su propia casa. CORPRENSA MI DIARIO- “NOS HACÍAN VENDER DESDE NIÑOS”: EL RELATO QUE EXPONE A PRESUNTA SECTA POWER MANTRA “YO CRECÍ PENSANDO QUE TODO ERA NORMAL” Uno de los elementos más impactantes de ambos testimonios es la normalización. “Yo crecí dentro de eso, así que crecí pensando que todo era normal”, expresó Shandrakanty Arevalo en un video enviado a este medio. La joven explicó que la organización se presentaba como una comunidad espiritual basada en meditación, vegetarianismo, yoga y prácticas hinduistas. “Las sectas no empiezan viéndose peligrosas. Primero parecen comunidades sanas, donde te reciben con amor, con atención y poco a poco empieza el control” , afirmó. Según ambos testimonios, desde niños fueron educados bajo la idea de que el mundo exterior era peligroso y que cuestionar las reglas del grupo era una forma de traición espiritual. “Nos enseñaban que el mundo de afuera era peligroso, que pensar por nosotros mismos era egoísmo y que querer independencia era algo malo” , dijo Shandrakanty. También aseguró que existían restricciones sobre amistades, redes sociales y relaciones personales. “No podíamos tener amistades fuera del grupo, no podíamos usar redes sociales libremente”. Kishorachandra, por su parte, relató que desde pequeño creció viendo imágenes del gurú dentro de su propia casa y que la figura del líder era presentada como un ser espiritualmente superior “Un casi Dios”, alguien a quien debían obediencia absoluta. Según contó a este medio, incluso llegó a vivir con el gurú en Panamá durante parte de su juventud. CORPRENSA MI DIARIO- “NOS HACÍAN VENDER DESDE NIÑOS”: EL RELATO QUE EXPONE A PRESUNTA SECTA POWER MANTRA EL GURÚ Y LA ESTRUCTURA DE PODER La organización, según describen documentos revisados por este medio, operaba bajo una estructura jerárquica rígida. El líder espiritual era tratado por los adeptos como una figura suprema y alrededor de él existían distintos niveles de control interno, incluyendo personas encargadas presuntamente de vigilancia, disciplina, finanzas y supervisión de las actividades comerciales. Dentro de esa estructura, una mujer conocida presuntamente bajo el alias de “La Pionera” habría tenido un rol importante en el control financiero, psicológico y operativo del grupo, según testimonios y documentos consultados por este medio. Las víctimas aseguran que la organización tenía presencia internacional y que anteriormente habría operado en países como Venezuela y República Dominicana antes de fortalecer su estructura en Panamá. Kishorachandra sostiene que, tras el inicio de la investigación en Panamá, varias redes sociales vinculadas presuntamente al grupo fueron eliminadas o desactivadas. CORPRENSA MI DIARIO- “NOS HACÍAN VENDER DESDE NIÑOS”: EL RELATO QUE EXPONE A PRESUNTA SECTA POWER MANTRA MENORES EN LAS CALLES Y CUOTAS DIARIAS Ambos hermanos coinciden en que desde menores de edad fueron enviados a vender productos en las calles de Panamá. Mi Diario conoció que las víctimas comercializaban principalmente joyería y otros artículos adquiridos en Zona Libre de Colón. “Desde menor de edad me mandaban a vender en las calles de Panamá”, afirmó Shandrakanty. Según sus testimonios, la actividad era presentada como una misión espiritual. “Nos decían que al vender estábamos rescatando almas caídas y llevando su energía al templo”. Kishorachandra explicó en entrevista con Telemetro Reporta que existía una cuota económica diaria. “Teníamos que llegar a 160 dólares diarios” , declaró. Aunque asegura que no existían castigos físicos directos, sí había presión psicológica constante y mecanismos de control interno. Según información recavada por este medio, cuando las víctimas no alcanzaban las metas podían recibir tareas adicionales de limpieza o restricciones internas. stories.title: UK police raid revives hunt for clues in German woman’s disappearance in India
stories.description: The family of a German woman who disappeared during a trip to India seven years ago say they hope a major police raid on a UK-based religious sect could help uncover what happened to her.
stories.articleText: UK police raid revives hunt for clues in German woman’s disappearance in India - The Times of India Edition IN IN US GCC English English हिन्दी मराठी ಕನ್ನಡ தமிழ் বাংলা മലയാളം తెలుగు ગુજરાતી Weather Sign In Today's ePaper Live News World News UK News UK police raid revives hunt for clues in German woman’s disappearance in India Trending Strait of Hormuz Chile Earthquake Abraham Accords Masoud Pezeshkian US Iran war Donald Trump warns Tehran Ebola Outbreak Strait of Hormuz Chile Earthquake Abraham Accords Masoud Pezeshkian US Iran war Donald Trump warns Tehran Ebola Outbreak Strait of Hormuz Chile Earthquake Abraham Accords Masoud Pezeshkian US Iran war Donald Trump warns Tehran Ebola Outbreak UK police raid revives hunt for clues in German woman’s disappearance in India TOI World Desk / TIMESOFINDIA.COM / Updated: May 26, 2026, 20:47 IST Comments Share AA + Text Size Small Medium Large UK Police The family of a German woman who disappeared during a trip to India seven years ago say they hope a major police raid on a UK-based religious sect could help uncover what happened to her. Lisa Wiese, who was 30 when she vanished in Kerala in March 2019, had been linked to the Ahmadi Religion of Peace and Light (AROPL), a sect headquartered in Crewe, Cheshire. According to The Guardian, the renewed attention follows a large-scale police operation in April in which about 500 officers raided the group’s headquarters and arrested 12 people on suspicion of offences including modern slavery, human trafficking , rape and forced marriage. All those arrested have since been released on bail pending further inquiries. Wiese disappeared days after arriving in India. Her family said the last contact came through emails and voice messages sent to relatives and her two children shortly before her Gmail account was deleted and her phone went silent. Her former husband, AbdelRahman Hashem, said he hoped the latest police investigation in the UK could help authorities revisit her disappearance and question individuals previously identified by German and Indian investigators. German and Indian police both opened missing persons investigations after Wiese vanished, but her family said progress had been limited over the years. Friends described Wiese as outgoing and compassionate. She converted to Islam in 2011 and later became involved with AROPL, a movement that blends Islamic teachings with conspiracy theories and alternative spiritual beliefs. After separating from her husband in 2017, Wiese moved between Germany and Sweden with members of the sect. In early 2019, her two children temporarily moved to live with their father in the United States while she discussed plans to rebuild her life. Wiese travelled to Kerala in March 2019 with assistance from members of the sect, though conflicting accounts later emerged about the purpose of the visit. Investigators believe she stayed briefly at a budget hotel near Varkala beach before disappearing. Authorities said there has been no activity on her bank accounts or phone since March 2019. Her family said the lack of answers has taken a heavy emotional toll, particularly on her children, who are now aged 11 and 12.
